required.title: [For Student] The 7 Habits of Highly Effective Teens
required.author: Sean Covey

required.title: [For Student] Grit: The Power of Passion and Perseverance
required.author: Angela Duckworth (selected chapters)

competence_markers.description: Does the student manage their own time and commitments without external reminders?
Do they prioritize effectively, putting important work before merely urgent tasks?
Is the quality of their work consistent whether or not someone is checking?
Can they perform to standard when motivation is low?
Are they mentoring a younger student with genuine investment and skill?
Can they self-assess honestly, identifying both strengths and weaknesses?

narration_prompts.prompt: Walk me through your time audit for this week.
narration_prompts.detail: Where did your time actually go? Where was the biggest gap between intention and reality? What will you change?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a situation where you had competing priorities and had to choose.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you choose? Why? What did you give up? Was it the right call?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is your internal standard for your work?
narration_prompts.detail: How do you know when you have met it? What happens when you notice you have fallen short and no one else has noticed?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you performed well even though you did not feel motivated.
narration_prompts.detail: What got you through it? What did you learn about the relationship between motivation and performance?

narration_prompts.prompt: How is your mentoring of a younger student going?
narration_prompts.detail: What have you learned about yourself through the experience? What is the hardest part?

narration_prompts.prompt: Conduct an honest self-assessment of the last month.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you do well? Where did you fall short? What specific changes will you make?

cross_connections.description: The executive function skills that are the cognitive infrastructure for self-management
The daily engine of execution — the discipline to perform regardless of feeling
The emotional resilience to handle setbacks, receive criticism, and maintain standards under pressure
Priority-setting is decision-making applied to your own life — the same tradeoff analysis used in all decisions
The self-managed student is ready to coordinate others — you cannot organize a team if you cannot organize yourself
Self-management is mission planning at the individual level — the same skills of objective definition, task decomposition, and progress tracking

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned the basics of self-leadership: follow through on your word, own your mistakes, take initiative, accept correction, manage your stuff. Those habits are still the foundation. But Weathering Storms asks more of you.

The shift is this: at Stretching Branches, adults structured your day, set your priorities, and reminded you when you forgot. You were learning to execute within a structure someone else built. At Weathering Storms, you begin to build the structure yourself. You manage your own time. You set your own priorities. You identify what needs to be done — not just what you have been assigned — and you manage your own execution without someone standing over you.

This is harder than it sounds. Most adults cannot do it well. They procrastinate, they get distracted by the urgent at the expense of the important, they overcommit and underdeliver, and they mistake being busy for making progress. You are learning these skills now so that by the time you are an adult, self-management is not something you struggle with — it is something you have been practicing for years.

The goal of this packet is to make you the kind of person who does not need to be managed. Not because you are rebellious or independent for its own sake, but because you have built an internal operating system — a set of habits, priorities, and standards — that keeps you on track without external supervision.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Time management as a discipline** — allocating time to commitments, tracking where time actually goes, and learning from the gap between intention and reality
2. **Priority-setting under competing demands** — deciding what matters most when everything seems urgent
3. **Proactive initiative on significant tasks** — identifying problems and proposing solutions without waiting for assignment
4. **Internal quality standards** — maintaining excellence whether or not anyone is watching
5. **Energy and motivation management** — performing consistently regardless of mood or feeling
6. **Mentoring younger students** — the first real leadership role, requiring patience, communication, and commitment to someone else's growth
7. **Self-assessment and course correction** — honestly evaluating your own performance and adjusting
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Time — The One Resource You Cannot Get Back

You have exactly the same number of hours in a day as every other person who has ever lived. The difference between people who accomplish what matters to them and people who feel perpetually behind is not talent, intelligence, or luck. It is how they manage those hours.

Time management is not about squeezing every minute for productivity. It is about alignment — making sure that how you actually spend your time matches what you say matters to you. Most people have never done this audit. They say their goals are important, but when you look at how they spend their hours, the goals get whatever time is left over after distractions, entertainment, and low-value tasks have consumed the day.

Here is the exercise: for one week, track how you actually spend your time. Not how you think you spend it — how you actually spend it. Write it down in thirty-minute blocks. At the end of the week, look at the record. How much time went to your most important work? How much went to distraction? How much was simply unaccounted for — time that passed without anything meaningful happening?

Most people who do this exercise are shocked. The gap between how they think they spend their time and how they actually spend it is enormous. This gap is the first thing to close.

Once you see reality clearly, you can start managing it. The basic discipline is this: at the beginning of each day (or the night before), identify the most important things that must happen. Not everything on your list — the most important things. Put those first. Protect the time for them. Do not let less important tasks, social distractions, or the feeling of not wanting to start push the important work to the end of the day when your energy is lowest and your time is shortest.

This is what adults mean when they talk about "eating the frog" — doing the hardest, most important thing first, before you have had time to build up resistance to it. The practice is simple. The discipline is not. But the discipline gets easier every time you do it, and the results compound: a person who consistently does the most important thing first, day after day, accomplishes more in a year than most people accomplish in five.

### Part 2: Priority-Setting — When Everything Seems Urgent

You will reach a point — and if you are developing well in this framework, you have probably already reached it — where you have more things to do than time to do them. Multiple commitments compete. A project is due, your mentor expects a narration, you promised to help a younger student, and you also need to practice your instrument. What do you do?

The temptation is to do the easiest thing first, or the most fun thing, or the thing that someone is actively bothering you about. None of these are good criteria for prioritization.

Better criteria: What is most important? What has the tightest deadline? What has consequences if it slips? What depends on other people (meaning they are waiting for your piece before they can continue)? What can only be done now versus what can be done later?

There is a classic framework for this: the urgent-important matrix. Tasks can be urgent (time-sensitive) or not urgent. They can be important (high impact, aligned with your goals) or not important. The mistake most people make is spending all their time on urgent-but-not-important tasks — things that feel pressing but do not actually matter — while neglecting important-but-not-urgent tasks — things that matter enormously but do not have a deadline screaming at them.

Examples of important-but-not-urgent: building a skill through daily practice, reading deeply, maintaining relationships, planning ahead, working on long-term projects. These are the things that build your future. They are also the things that are easiest to skip today because nothing bad happens today if you skip them. The consequences show up weeks or months later, when the skill is not built, the project is not done, or the relationship has eroded.

Learning to prioritize the important over the merely urgent is one of the most valuable self-leadership skills you will ever develop. It requires the ability to say no to some things — even good things — in order to say yes to the most important things. And saying no is a skill that most people never learn.

### Part 3: The Standard Is Yours

At Stretching Branches, your standard was largely set by adults. They told you what "done" looked like. They checked your work. They reminded you when it slipped. At Weathering Storms, the standard starts to become yours.

Here is the test: what is the quality of your work when nobody is watching? When no one is going to check, when there is no grade, when the only person who will ever know whether you gave full effort is you — what do you do?

A person whose standard depends on external oversight is a person who works for approval. When the approval goes away — when the audience disappears — the quality drops. This person will be a mediocre employee, a mediocre business owner, and a mediocre leader, because their standard is borrowed, not owned.

A person whose standard is internal does the same quality work whether or not anyone is watching. They do it because sloppy work bothers them. They do it because cutting corners feels wrong, not because someone will catch them. They do it because they have decided what kind of person they are, and that decision does not change based on who is in the room.

How do you build an internal standard? You start by noticing the difference between your best work and your acceptable work. Then you make a decision: which one is your standard? If your standard is your best work — not perfection, but genuine best effort — then that is what you produce every time. When you catch yourself cutting corners, you notice it, and you correct it. Not because of guilt, but because of identity. "I am a person who does excellent work" is a statement about who you are, and every time you act on it, it becomes more true.

### Part 4: Performing Without Motivation

There is a myth — especially popular in modern culture — that you should only work when you feel motivated. That you need to find your passion, wait for inspiration, or get into the right mood before you can do your best work. This is false. It is catastrophically false for anyone who wants to accomplish anything significant.

Motivation is unreliable. It comes and goes based on sleep, mood, weather, social dynamics, and a hundred other factors you cannot control. If you wait for motivation to work, you will work sporadically and unpredictably. Your output will be a function of how you feel, not what you committed to.

The alternative is discipline. Discipline means performing to your standard regardless of how you feel. You practice the instrument when you do not feel like practicing. You write the narration when you would rather be outside. You show up to your commitment when you are tired, frustrated, or bored. Not because suffering is the goal — but because the things that matter most in your life cannot be built only in the moments when you feel like building them.

This does not mean you should ignore your energy and emotional state. Effective self-leaders know when they are at their best and schedule their most important work for those times. They know when they need rest and they rest deliberately, not guiltily. They know the difference between genuine exhaustion (which requires recovery) and ordinary reluctance (which requires discipline to override).

The distinction matters: discipline is not grinding yourself into dust. It is performing to standard consistently, managing your energy intelligently, and not letting the fluctuations of feeling dictate the reliability of your output.

### Part 5: Mentoring — Your First Leadership Role

At some point in Weathering Storms, you will be given responsibility for a younger student's learning or development in a specific area. This is your first real leadership role, and it will teach you more about leadership than any book or discussion.

When you mentor a younger student, you immediately discover things about yourself. You discover how patient you actually are — not how patient you think you are, but how patient you are when a seven-year-old asks you the same question for the fourth time. You discover whether you can explain something clearly — not in the way that makes sense to you, but in the way that makes sense to them. You discover whether you can stay committed to someone else's progress when it is slow, frustrating, and less interesting than your own work.

Mentoring requires you to put someone else's needs ahead of your own convenience. The younger student needs your attention right now, and you would rather be doing something else. They need you to explain it one more time, and you are frustrated. They are not learning as fast as you think they should, and you are tempted to just do it for them. Every one of these moments is a leadership test. Do you serve the person you are responsible for, or do you serve your own comfort?

The best mentors do several things consistently: they are patient without being condescending. They explain clearly and check for understanding. They adjust their approach when the first explanation does not land. They celebrate genuine progress without offering false praise. And they care about the outcome — they are invested in the younger student's growth, not just going through the motions.

### Part 6: Honest Self-Assessment

The most advanced self-leadership skill at Weathering Storms is the ability to evaluate your own performance honestly. Not the performance you wish you had delivered. Not the performance you would describe to someone trying to impress them. Your actual performance.

This requires a specific kind of honesty that most people avoid for their entire lives. It requires you to ask: Where am I cutting corners? Where is the gap between my stated standard and my actual performance? What am I avoiding? What weakness am I protecting instead of addressing?

A regular practice: at the end of each week, review how the week actually went. Not how busy you were — busy is not the same as effective. What did you commit to? What did you deliver? Where did the gap appear? What will you do differently next week?

This practice is the foundation of continuous improvement. A person who does it honestly, week after week, improves steadily and substantially. A person who avoids it stays where they are — or slowly degrades without noticing, because they never look honestly at the trajectory.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Self-Directed Time Management
**Developing well:** The student plans their day or week proactively. They allocate time to priorities before reactive tasks. They can identify when time was wasted and adjust. They are increasingly independent in managing their schedule.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot account for where time goes. They are consistently reactive — responding to whatever is in front of them rather than executing a plan. Time management only happens when an adult structures it for them.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student done the time audit? Do they have a physical or written planning system? Are they given enough autonomy to actually manage their own time, or is the schedule entirely imposed?

### 2. Priority-Setting Under Competing Demands
**Developing well:** The student can articulate why they chose one task over another. They distinguish between urgent and important. They can say no to lower-priority requests. They handle competing demands without paralysis or meltdown.

**Developing slowly:** The student defaults to the easiest, most fun, or most recently assigned task. They cannot articulate why they chose what they chose. When demands conflict, they freeze, complain, or do nothing.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student genuinely facing competing demands, or has the schedule been simplified to remove all conflict? Some friction is necessary for the skill to develop. Are you modeling priority-setting visibly in your own work?

### 3. Internal Quality Standards
**Developing well:** The quality of work is consistent whether supervised or not. The student notices when their own work falls short and corrects it before being told. They can articulate what their standard is and hold themselves to it.

**Developing slowly:** Quality varies dramatically with supervision level. The student produces excellent work when watched and mediocre work when unsupervised. They do not self-correct.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the standard been explicitly defined? Does the student know what excellent looks like in their specific tasks? Is there a pattern of praising the student only for outcomes (which teaches them to perform for an audience) versus praising the process and standard (which teaches internal ownership)?

### 4. Mentoring Effectiveness
**Developing well:** The younger student is making genuine progress. The mentor-student communicates clearly, adjusts approach based on the younger student's needs, demonstrates patience, and is invested in the outcome. They can narrate what is working and what is not in their mentoring.

**Developing slowly:** The mentoring relationship is superficial — the student goes through motions without investment. The younger student is not progressing. The student-mentor becomes frustrated easily, does tasks for the younger student, or avoids the responsibility.

**Diagnostic questions:** Was the mentoring match appropriate? Is the domain one where the student has genuine competence and the younger student has genuine need? Has the student received any guidance on how to mentor, or were they simply assigned the role and expected to figure it out?
